title: Nils-Christian Bormann
text:
Department of Philosophy, Politics, and Economics · Witten/Herdecke University

I am Professor of International Political Studies in the Department of Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at Witten/Herdecke University and an affiliate at the Politics of Inequality Cluster at the University of Konstanz. I study political violence, ethnic politics, power-sharing coalitions, and democratic survival. My research has been published in the American Journal of Political Science, the American Political Science Review, the Journal of Politics, and has been supported by the European Research Council.

title: Conflict Research Exchange Workshop (CREW)
text: Founded in 2022, the Conflict Research Exchange Workshop (CREW) brings together political scientists from the University of Osnabrück, the University of Duisburg-Essen, and Witten/Herdecke University to discuss ongoing research and new ideas. It aims to give regular feedback to its participants, in particular to emerging scholars at the PhD and post-doctoral levels in the fields of conflict research and contentious politics. Online workshops take place monthly. Once a year, we organize a joint retreat.

title: Conflict Research Exchange Workshop (CREW)
text:
Members




Alexander De Juan


Professor of Comparative Politics, University of Osnabrück


Alexander De Juan’s research focuses on political attitudes and behavior in the context of conflict and autocratic rule with a particular interest in historical episodes of contention.





Finn Klebe


Post-Doc, University of Osnabrück


Finn Klebe’s research examines contentious politics, repression, and civil war dynamics, focusing on organizational relations and their impact on conflict escalation, democratization, and post-conflict political attitudes.





Johannes Vüllers


Professor of International Relations, University of Duisburg-Essen


Johannes Vüllers studies contentious politics, civil wars, and conflict resolution with a regional expertise on Southeast Asia.





Nils-Christian Bormann


Professor of International Political Studies, Witten/Herdecke University


Nils-Christian Bormann investigates the causes and consequences of political violence, power-sharing coalitions, and group-based inequality.





Olga Jerjomina


PhD Student, Witten/Herdecke University


Olga Jerjomina studies ethnic identities and political behaviour, examining how group-based divisions structure political attitudes, voting patterns, and susceptibility to external political influence.





Teresa Hummler


Post-Doc, University of Duisburg Essen


Teresa Hummler is studying the influence of contentious politics on organizational development and individual political attitudes. In her PhD thesis, she investigated how neighborhood characteristics shape individuals’ political support.

title: Data
text:
Citizen Anger Interwar News (CAIN) Data


The Citizen Anger Interwar News (CAIN) Data covers political violence events reports from ten European interwar democracies. It records their location, timing, the number of casualties, and actors involved. The interactive map allows you to explore the data.


Dataset Interactive Map







Democratic Electoral Systems


Information on electoral rules in 1,826 legislative elections and 648 presidential elections in democracies since 1919. The dataset spans two time periods covered in separate data publications: the interwar period (1919–1945) and the postwar period (1946–2020).


Interwar Data (1919–1945) Postwar Data (1946–2020)







Archive of Interwar Europe Election Data & Assemblies (AIEEDA)


A multi-level dataset of parliamentary elections in interwar Europe (1919–1939), covering 137 elections across 25 democracies. Includes electoral results, ideological and organizational data for 401 parties and 35 alliances, and cabinet data.


Dataset Paper







Ethnic Power Relations (EPR)


Data on ethnic groups’ access to state power, their settlement patterns, links to rebel organizations, and transborder ethnic kin. The EPR dataset family is maintained by the International Conflict Research group at ETH Zürich.


Dataset







Linking Ethnic Data from Africa (LEDA)


A systematic approach to linking over 8,100 ethnic categories from 11 databases on ethnic groups in Africa. Includes the LEDA open-source software package to help researchers merge ethnic data across datasets.


Paper PDF

title: Teaching
text:
Below you find some of the classes I teach regularly. I am open to supervising BA, MA, or PhD theses with an empirical focus. If you are interested in working with me, prepare a short overview of the research question you are interested in, and how you would like to investigate it.
If you would like to discuss teaching or study-related issues please sign up for office hours here.

title: Measuring the Ethno-Linguistic Fractionalization (ELF) Index at the Micro-Level
text:
In their influential 1997 Quarterly Journal of Economics study, Easterly and Levine describe the Ethno-Linguistic Fractionalization (ELF) index as “a measure of ethnolinguistic diversity … that measures the probability that two randomly selected individuals in a country belong to different ethnolinguistic group.” A complement of the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) of concentration, the ELF is one of the most widely used metrics in the social sciences, employed in foundational studies of civil war, development, growth, and party systems. In political science, Laakso and Taagepera’s effective number of parties (ENP) measure, the inverse of the HHI, is similarly important. Despite its clear micro-foundations, social scientists are using the ELF and related indices as aggregate measurements at the national, sub-national, or local level.
In a recently published study in the American Political Science Review, Carl Müller-Crepon and I show that the ELF, the ENP, and other indices, such as the GINI, can be estimated at the micro-level. We rely on a widely-used data source in the social sciences: nationally representative probability samples. Rather than analyzing individual-level survey data directly, we pair respondents into dyads and assess their likeness on variables such as ethnicity or (intended) vote choice. This gives us a nationally representative set of two randomly selected individuals, who belong to the same or different ethnic groups or voted for the same or different political parties. The sample mean of these estimates approaches the country-level index of interest.1
Our pairwise, survey-based approach to index measurement offers several advantages. First, the ELF estimated on respondent-pair data comes with uncertainty estimates that an index calculated with aggregate-level population share data lacks. Second, estimating the ELF on respondent pairs from surveys dramatically increases statistical power. Third, a pairwise approach enables the simultaneous analysis of micro, meso, and macro patterns. Fourth, estimating the ELF at the respondent-pair-level opens up broad comparisons across different contexts without sacrificing individual-level insights.
To realize these advantages, we derive the new covoting regression (CVR) model. The CVR is a simple linear probability model estimated on pairs of survey-respondents (see Figure). By focusing on covoting and coethnicity rather than individual voting intentions and ethno-linguistic identity, we abstract away from country-specific idiosyncrasies. This abstraction allows us to compare the effect of cleavages like ethnicity on voting intentions over time or across countries, even if voters face different parties in different elections or the range of relevant identity categories varies.
Furthermore, the CVR addresses two methodological challenges that plagued earlier approaches to studying the relationship between ethnicity and voting intentions. First, existing micro-level studies that built on survey data suffered from potential selection bias because voters’ choices are frequently limited. For example, voters might be faced with a menu that consists exclusively of (non-)coethnic candidates. Existing studies that define ethnic voting as the correspondence of voter and candidate identity will over(under)-estimate ethnic voting. By focusing on coethnicity and shared voting intentions, the CVR captures bloc-voting pattern, where most members of one group support the same candidate or party.2 Second, the CVR avoids potential ecological fallacies and resulting omitted variable bias that potentially arise in aggregate group or country-level analyses. This problem threatens to become acute in cases where cross-cleavage covoting is wide-spread.
In our study, we apply the CVR to studying voting intentions in 28 sub-Saharan Africa countries using five waves of Afrobarometer surveys. Our approach allows us to comparatively revisit multiple questions that scholars have asked in single-case studies. We investigate the relative importance of coethnicity in voting intentions as compared to other cleavages, if and how the effect of coethnicity changes over time in different contexts, and the mechanisms by which coethnicity affects vote choice.
We conclude with three key findings from our study:

title: Measuring the Ethno-Linguistic Fractionalization (ELF) Index at the Micro-Level
text:
Footnotes


Sometimes a simple transformation like taking the complement in case of the ELF, or the inverse in case of the ENP, is necessary.↩︎
The CVR will still be biased if such restricted choice menus result from strategic party entry or exit, but its bias is smaller than that of alternative models.↩︎
